Root Treatise of the Middle Way
MūlamadhyamakakārikāExamination of Conditions — Examination of Views · Complete in Twenty-Seven Chapters
A River of Logic from Dependent Origination to Emptiness — From the General Outline of the “Eight Negations” to “The True Nature of All Phenomena,” All Twenty-Seven Chapters in Sequence
Composed by Nāgārjuna Bodhisattva · Commentary by the Brahmin Piṅgala · Translated (into Chinese) by Tripiṭaka Master Kumārajīva of the Later Qin
Nāgārjuna Bodhisattva’s Root Treatise on the Middle Way · Volumes I–IV · All Twenty-Seven Chapters
This is a personal study compilation, warmly shared as a connection to the Dharma.
不生亦不滅，不常亦不斷；
不一亦不異，不來亦不出。
能說是因緣，善滅諸戲論；
我稽首禮佛，諸說中第一。
Editor’s Preface

The Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (中論, “Treatise on the Middle Way”; the Sanskrit title means “Root Verses on the Middle Way”) is Nāgārjuna Bodhisattva’s representative work, and the foundational treatise of the Mahāyāna Madhyamaka school. The Chinese Buddhist tradition relies on the edition translated by Tripiṭaka Master Kumārajīva of the Later Qin, together with the commentary of Piṅgala, comprising twenty-seven chapters in total, traditionally divided into four volumes. This reading edition compiles all twenty-seven chapters into a single volume, fully preserving each chapter’s original sections and internal table of contents, so that readers may take in the full arc of the treatise at a glance.

The treatise as a whole is not a scattered collection of twenty-seven separate topics, but a tightly reasoned river of logic advancing step by step. Volume I raises the general outline of dependent origination and emptiness through the verse of the “Eight Negations,” then deconstructs body, mind, and world layer by layer through the examinations of motion, the six sense faculties, the five aggregates, the six elements, and desire and the desirous. Volume II turns to subtler objects — the three characteristics of conditioned things, the agent, and the prior-existing self that allow all phenomena to be established, together with suffering, formations, and contact. Volume III probes directly into “intrinsic nature,” the ultimate root of all conceptual proliferation, and arrives at the pivot of the entire treatise in the Examination of Self and Phenomena, which directly reveals “the true nature of all phenomena, tranquil and free of conceptual proliferation.” Volume IV examines the Tathāgata and erroneous views, answers the challenge that “if everything is empty, then the Four Noble Truths would all collapse” in the weightiest chapter of the treatise, the Examination of the Four Noble Truths, and finally clarifies nirvāṇa, the twelve links of dependent origination, and wrong views, bringing the entire treatise to its completion.

For ease of cross-reference, each chapter opens with a bridging “Chapter Summary” explaining the core question that chapter sets out to resolve, and closes with a bridging “Looking Back, Looking Forward” passage explaining how the previous chapter concluded and why the next chapter takes up its new topic. The main text of each chapter fully preserves its original form and internal table of contents, and readers may navigate freely via the sidebar and the master table of contents. May the reader follow this river of logic all the way to Nāgārjuna’s Middle Way reality of “ultimate emptiness, and yet vividly, functionally existent.”

Master Table of Contents

Volume I · Chapter 1
Ch. 1: Examination of Conditions
The Eight Negations: A Summary of Dependent Origination — Refuting the Four Conditions to Establish the Root of Emptiness
Chapter Summary

The opening chapter of the Treatise on the Middle Way. Nāgārjuna sums up the entire treatise’s purpose in the verse of Eight Negations — ‘Neither arising nor ceasing, neither permanent nor annihilated, neither identical nor different, neither coming nor going’ — and then, one by one, refutes the claim that the four types of conditions (primary cause, sequential condition, object condition, and dominant condition) can truly produce an effect. This establishes the treatise’s foundational position: because all things arise from conditions, they have no intrinsic nature, and their fundamental nature is emptiness.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the Eight Negations established, the principle of dependent origination and emptiness has been outlined in full. Yet what people find hardest to release is what they can see with their own eyes: motion. Going, coming, walking — these appear vividly real before us, so how could they not truly exist? The next chapter, Examination of Motion, takes this most intuitive of phenomena as its target, demonstrating how Middle Way reasoning deconstructs the intrinsic nature of ‘going’ itself.

Next: Chapter 2 · Examination of Motion →
Volume I · Chapter 2
Ch. 2: Examination of Motion
The Vajra Sword of Wisdom: Deconstructing the Intrinsic Nature of Time, Motion, and the Goer
Chapter Summary

Having established the framework of dependent origination and emptiness in the previous chapter, this chapter turns to the most compelling intuition of ‘true existence’: motion. Nāgārjuna applies a threefold analysis — ‘In what has been gone there is no going; in what has not been gone there is also no going; apart from what has been gone and what has not been gone, there is no going in the act of going either’ — to refute the intrinsic nature of the act of going, the goer, and the time of going. This shows that even the most vivid phenomenon of movement is utterly without substance.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With motion shown to be empty, the process of ‘that which moves’ has been deconstructed. But what, then, of the faculties that see and hear? People still feel that there is a real ‘eye’ doing the seeing. The next chapter, Examination of the Six Senses, turns its blade to the six sense faculties, beginning with the point that ‘the eye cannot see itself’ to refute the reality of the perceiving subject.

Next: Chapter 3 · Examination of the Six Senses →
Volume I · Chapter 3
Ch. 3: Examination of the Six Senses
From ‘The Eye Cannot See Itself’ to the Logic of Liberation Through No-Self
Chapter Summary

With motion refuted, this chapter turns to the organs of perception — the six sense faculties of eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. Nāgārjuna pivots on the argument that ‘if the eye itself cannot see itself, how can it see other things?’ — showing that if the faculty of seeing has no intrinsic nature, then the seeing, the seen, and the seer are all equally unobtainable.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

The ‘seeing’ of the six sense faculties has been shown to be empty. Yet people still cling to the idea that the aggregation of the five skandhas that are known truly constitutes a self. The next chapter, Examination of the Five Aggregates, delves into form, sensation, perception, formations, and consciousness, refuting the truly-existent relationship between the aggregates and their causes.

Next: Chapter 4 · Examination of the Five Aggregates →
Volume I · Chapter 4
Ch. 4: Examination of the Five Aggregates
The Weave of Emptiness: Deconstructing Form, Sensation, Perception, Formations, and Consciousness in Relation to Their Causes
Chapter Summary

With the six sense faculties refuted, this chapter examines the five aggregates that constitute body and mind: form, sensation, perception, formations, and consciousness. Nāgārjuna begins with the argument that ‘apart from the cause of form, form cannot be found; apart from form, the cause of form cannot be found,’ revealing that the aggregates and their causes can neither be separated from nor identified with one another. The aggregation of the five skandhas is therefore not a genuine basis for a real self.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

The notion that the five aggregates provide a truly existent basis has been refuted. Still, the mind retreats one step further: even if the aggregates are not real, surely the six elements — earth, water, fire, wind, space, and consciousness — that underlie all things must be real ‘substance’? The next chapter, Examination of the Elements, uses space as its example to refute the six elements.

Next: Chapter 5 · Examination of the Elements →
Volume I · Chapter 5
Ch. 5: Examination of the Elements
Seeing Through the Logical Puzzle of ‘Substance’ and ‘Characteristic’ by Way of Space
Chapter Summary

With the five aggregates refuted, this chapter refutes the six elements that constitute the world: earth, water, fire, wind, space, and consciousness. Nāgārjuna takes ‘space’ as his specific example: ‘Before the characteristic of space exists, there is no phenomenon of space’ — the characteristic and the phenomenon depend on each other to be established, and neither can exist independently. All six elements are thus without intrinsic nature.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

The ‘substance’ that constitutes the world has been shown to be empty. With both the objective world and the sense organs refuted, the most intimate question now arises: are afflictive emotions — desire and attachment — and the ‘person who feels them’ truly existent? The next chapter, Examination of Desire and the Desirous, refutes both desire and the desirous.

Next: Chapter 6 · Examination of Desire and the Desirous →
Volume I · Chapter 6
Ch. 6: Examination of Desire and the Desirous
A Dialectical Examination of the Real Existence of Emotion and Self
Chapter Summary

With the objective world refuted, this chapter turns to subjective emotion: desire (the afflictive state) and the one who desires (the afflicted subject). Nāgārjuna’s analysis — ‘If, apart from the afflictive state, the afflicted one existed first, then it would be because of this desirous one that the afflictive state arises’ — shows that desire and the desirous cannot be sequential, nor can they be identical or different. Neither emotion nor self has any substance. Volume I is thus complete.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Volume I moves from the general outline of dependent origination to the refutation of the emotional subject. Entering Volume II, Nāgārjuna turns his focus to a subtler target: the three characteristics of arising, abiding, and ceasing that allow all ‘conditioned phenomena’ to be established. The next chapter, Examination of the Conditioned, refutes these three characteristics.

Next: Chapter 7 · Examination of the Conditioned →
Volume II · Chapter 7
Ch. 7: Examination of the Conditioned
Unraveling the Logical Labyrinth of Time and Existence: Refuting the Three Characteristics of Conditioned Things
Chapter Summary

The beginning of Volume II. All ‘conditioned phenomena’ are taken to be real precisely because they possess the three characteristics of arising, abiding, and ceasing. This chapter poses the dilemma: ‘If arising is itself conditioned, it should have three characteristics of its own; if arising is unconditioned, how can it be called a characteristic of the conditioned?’ — and through successive layers of inquiry into ‘the arising of arising’ and ‘the original arising,’ thoroughly dismantles the intrinsic nature of the three characteristics.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the three characteristics of the conditioned refuted, ‘that which is produced’ is shown to be unreal. But what, then, of ‘the one who produces’? People still feel there must be a real subject who ‘acts.’ The next chapter, Examination of Action and Agent, refutes the mutually dependent clinging to action and agent.

Next: Chapter 8 · Examination of Action and Agent →
Volume II · Chapter 8
Ch. 8: Examination of Action and Agent
The True Nature of Cause and Effect: Deconstructing Action and Subject
Chapter Summary

With the three characteristics refuted, this chapter examines the structure of action: the deed (the act itself) and the agent (the subject who acts). Through the exhaustive analysis that ‘a determinate agent does not perform a determinate deed; an indeterminate agent does not perform an indeterminate deed,’ Nāgārjuna shows that agent and deed depend on each other, and neither can be established independently and prior to the other.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Both agent and deed are shown to be empty. Yet the mind retreats further still: even if the agent is not real, surely there must be a self that ‘exists prior to seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing’? The next chapter, Examination of the Prior Existent, refutes this notion of a ‘pre-existing’ self.

Next: Chapter 9 · Examination of the Prior Existent →
Volume II · Chapter 9
Ch. 9: Examination of the Prior Existent
Refuting the Assumption of a Pre-Existing Self Prior to Seeing and Hearing
Chapter Summary

With action and agent refuted, this chapter targets the deepest form of self-clinging: the ‘prior existent,’ a self supposed to exist independently, prior to seeing, hearing, sensation, and awareness. Nāgārjuna’s analysis — ‘If a prior existent existed apart from the eye, ear, and so forth, then eye, ear, and so forth should also exist apart from the prior existent’ — shows that the prior existent and the sense faculties cannot be separated from one another. A pre-existing self is thus utterly unobtainable.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the ‘pre-existing self’ refuted, Nāgārjuna draws on a vivid analogy to bring the refutation of self-clinging to a close. The next chapter, Examination of Fire and Fuel, uses the analogy of fire and fuel to show that the self and the five aggregates are neither identical nor different.

Next: Chapter 10 · Examination of Fire and Fuel →
Volume II · Chapter 10
Ch. 10: Examination of Fire and Fuel
The Mutual Dependence of Fire and Fuel: Refuting the Clinging to Sameness or Difference Between Self and the Five Aggregates
Chapter Summary

Following the previous chapter’s refutation of the ‘prior existent,’ this chapter uses ‘fire’ as an analogy for the self and ‘fuel’ as an analogy for the five aggregates, examining the relationship between them. Nāgārjuna’s twofold refutation — ‘If fire were identical to fuel, agent and act would be one and the same; if fire were different from fuel, fire should exist apart from fuel’ — shows that self and the five aggregates are neither identical nor different. Self-clinging is left with no ground to stand on.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Having closed the refutation of self-clinging with the analogy of fire, the perspective now widens to the great river of time: could birth-and-death and saṃsāra have a starting point? The next chapter, Examination of the Prior Limit, refutes the notion of an original starting point of birth and death.

Next: Chapter 11 · Examination of the Prior Limit →
Volume II · Chapter 11
Ch. 11: Examination of the Prior Limit
The Ultimate Logic for Dissolving the Illusion of Birth and Death
Chapter Summary

With the emptiness of self now made clear through the analogy of fire and fuel, this chapter extends the analysis to time: does the flow of birth and death in saṃsāra have an original starting point (a ‘prior limit’)? Citing the verse ‘As the great sage has said, the prior limit cannot be found,’ Nāgārjuna uses the dilemma of which comes first, birth or death, to show that saṃsāra has no beginning, and the prior limit is ultimately unobtainable.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the prior limit of birth and death shown to be unobtainable, what then of the central experience of this cycle — suffering? Where does it arise from? Is it self-made, other-made, made by both, or made without cause? The next chapter, Examination of Suffering, refutes all four possible sources of suffering.

Next: Chapter 12 · Examination of Suffering →
Volume II · Chapter 12
Ch. 12: Examination of Suffering
Deconstructing the Logic of Attributing Suffering to a Cause: Refuting Four Fallacies and Reconstructing the Perspective of Dependent Origination
Chapter Summary

With the prior limit refuted, this chapter focuses on the most intimate of experiences: suffering. Nāgārjuna exhaustively lists and refutes, one by one, the four possibilities that ‘suffering is self-made, other-made, made by both, or made without cause,’ showing that suffering has no real cause to which it can be attributed — it is simply dependently arisen. And what is true of suffering holds equally for external phenomena.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the attribution of suffering to a cause now deconstructed, a more general question follows: do all ‘formations’ — the ever-changing, conditioned phenomena of activity — possess any real, unchanging essence? The next chapter, Examination of Formations, refutes the intrinsic nature of all formations.

Next: Chapter 13 · Examination of Formations →
Volume II · Chapter 13
Ch. 13: Examination of Formations
The Logic of Emptiness: Establishing That All Formations Are Deceptive and Without Intrinsic Nature
Chapter Summary

Following the previous chapter’s refutation regarding suffering, this chapter extends the analysis to all ‘formations’ (conditioned, constructed phenomena). Through the arguments ‘because formations are grasped through delusion, they are called deceptive’ and ‘because phenomena are subject to change, they are known to be without intrinsic nature,’ Nāgārjuna establishes from both the angle of delusion and the angle of change that all formations lack intrinsic nature — while also refuting the error of clinging to ‘emptiness’ itself as something truly existent.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With all formations shown to be empty, and emptiness itself not to be clung to, what then of the ‘contact’ that occurs when phenomena come together — as when the seeing, the seen, and the seer meet to form perception? Is this ‘contact’ truly existent? The next chapter, Examination of Contact, refutes the characteristic of contact.

Next: Chapter 14 · Examination of Contact →
Volume II · Chapter 14
Ch. 14: Examination of Contact
Refuting the Coming-Together of the Seeing, the Seen, and the Seer
Chapter Summary

With all formations shown to be empty, this chapter examines ‘contact’ — the meeting of the seeing, the seen, and the seer that gives rise to perception. Nāgārjuna’s argument — ‘Seeing, the visible, and the seer are three mutually distinct things; being thus distinct, these three phenomena can never come together’ — shows that things which are different cannot come into contact, while things which are the same have no need to; the characteristic of contact is therefore unobtainable. Volume II is thus complete.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Volume II has, in turn, refuted the conditioned, the agent, the self, suffering, formations, and contact. Entering Volume III, Nāgārjuna probes directly into the ultimate root of all conceptual proliferation: intrinsic nature itself. The next chapter, Examination of Being and Non-Being, directly refutes intrinsic nature, extrinsic nature, being, and non-being.

Next: Chapter 15 · Examination of Being and Non-Being →
Volume III · Chapter 15
Ch. 15: Examination of Being and Non-Being
Unraveling the Dialectical Calculus of Reality: A Direct Refutation of Intrinsic Nature and the Two Views of Being and Non-Being
Chapter Summary

The beginning of Volume III, and the theoretical core of the entire treatise. This chapter dissects ‘intrinsic nature’ directly: ‘For an intrinsic nature to exist within conditions is simply not so; an intrinsic nature that arose from conditions would be a produced thing.’ Since intrinsic nature cannot arise from conditions, and yet cannot be said to be non-existent either, Nāgārjuna uses this to refute both the view of ‘being’ and the view of ‘non-being,’ thereby revealing the Middle Way.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Intrinsic nature, extrinsic nature, being, and non-being have all been refuted, and the root of conceptual proliferation has been severed. Yet the deepest fear of sentient beings is being ‘bound within saṃsāra, while somehow still able to attain liberation.’ The next chapter, Examination of Bondage and Liberation, refutes the true existence of both bondage and liberation.

Next: Chapter 16 · Examination of Bondage and Liberation →
Volume III · Chapter 16
Ch. 16: Examination of Bondage and Liberation
The Ultimate Logic for Dispelling Saṃsāra and Bondage
Chapter Summary

With intrinsic nature refuted, this chapter examines the structure of saṃsāra and liberation: who is bound, and who is liberated? Through the arguments ‘that which transmigrates through the formations should not transmigrate if permanent, nor should it if impermanent’ and ‘the bound has no liberation, and the unbound has no liberation either,’ Nāgārjuna shows that both bondage and liberation are unobtainable, and that birth-and-death and nirvāṇa are not two separate things.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With bondage and liberation shown to be empty, the difficulty that immediately follows is this: if there is no truly existent bound being, how do good and evil ‘karma’ continue to flow, and how are their results not lost? The next chapter, Examination of Karma, refutes the intrinsic nature of karma while preserving the non-loss of karmic results.

Next: Chapter 17 · Examination of Karma →
Volume III · Chapter 17
Ch. 17: Examination of Karma
Deconstructing the Logic of Karmic Force, from ‘True Existence’ to ‘Illusion-Like’
Chapter Summary

With bondage and liberation refuted, this chapter confronts the thorniest of problems: karma and its results. Nāgārjuna comprehensively refutes the various constructions proposed to explain how ‘karma possesses intrinsic nature’ (such as the ‘imperishable factor,’ continuity, and the analogy of a bond or contract), concluding with: ‘Karma is like an illusion, like a dream, like a mirage, and like an echo’ — karma has no intrinsic nature, and yet cause and effect are not lost.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With karma shown to be illusion-like, the treatise has now, through this comprehensive refutation, dispelled the view of true existence from every angle. The next chapter, Examination of Self and Phenomena, is the pivot of the entire Treatise on the Middle Way — here Nāgārjuna no longer merely refutes, but directly reveals the correct view of ‘the true nature of all phenomena’ and ‘no-self.’

Next: Chapter 18 · Examination of Self and Phenomena →
Volume III · Chapter 18
Ch. 18: Examination of Self and Phenomena
An Ultimate Guide to Entering the True Nature of All Phenomena Through ‘No-Self’
Chapter Summary

This chapter is the pivotal turning point of the entire treatise. Nāgārjuna draws together the refutations of the previous seventeen chapters and directly reveals the true nature of all phenomena. ‘If the self were the five aggregates, the self would be subject to arising and ceasing’ refutes the self; ‘known through itself, not dependent on another, tranquil, free of conceptual proliferation, without distinction and without differentiation — this is called true reality’ reveals the truth. True reality is that in which all conceptual proliferation is stilled and the movement of the mind itself comes to rest.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the true nature of all phenomena now directly set forth, Nāgārjuna turns back to clear away a few remaining strongholds of the view of true existence. The first is ‘time’ — the seemingly real framework of past, present, and future. The next chapter, Examination of Time, refutes the intrinsic nature of time.

Next: Chapter 19 · Examination of Time →
Volume III · Chapter 19
Ch. 19: Examination of Time
Refuting the Mutually Dependent Intrinsic Nature of the Three Times: Past, Present, and Future
Chapter Summary

With true reality now made clear, this chapter turns back to clear away the remaining stronghold of ‘time.’ Through the analysis of mutual dependence — ‘if future and present time exist in dependence on past time, then future and present time should already exist within past time’ — Nāgārjuna shows that the three times are established only in mutual dependence on one another, and none can exist independently. Time is therefore without intrinsic nature.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With time shown to be empty, the notion of ‘causal continuity’ most closely related to time is next examined: is the process by which a cause ceases and an effect arises, in causal succession, truly real? The next chapter, Examination of Cause and Effect, refutes the claim that the coming-together of cause and conditions produces an effect.

Next: Chapter 20 · Examination of Cause and Effect →
Volume III · Chapter 20
Ch. 20: Examination of Cause and Effect
Middle Way Logic and the Method of Liberation: Seeing Through the Illusion-Like Reality of Dependent Origination
Chapter Summary

With time refuted, this chapter examines the relationship between cause and effect: can the coming-together of conditions truly ‘produce’ an effect? Through multiple lines of analysis, such as ‘if an effect arises from the coming-together of conditions, and it already existed within that coming-together, then what need was there for the coming-together to produce it,’ Nāgārjuna shows that neither the position that the effect pre-exists in the cause, nor the position that it does not, can be sustained. Cause and effect are thus illusion-like.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the arising and ceasing of cause and effect now deconstructed, a still larger question emerges: the ‘coming-into-being and destruction’ of the world itself. Are becoming and destruction truly existent? The next chapter, Examination of Becoming and Destruction, refutes the characteristics of becoming and destruction.

Next: Chapter 21 · Examination of Becoming and Destruction →
Volume III · Chapter 21
Ch. 21: Examination of Becoming and Destruction
A Deconstruction of the Mutual Dependence of Becoming and Destruction: No Real Becoming or Destruction Within the Flow of Arising and Ceasing
Chapter Summary

With cause and effect refuted, this chapter broadens its scope to the ‘becoming’ (coming into being) and ‘destruction’ (dissolution) of the world and of life. Nāgārjuna’s analysis of mutual dependence — ‘apart from becoming, and together with becoming, there is no destruction within it; apart from destruction, and together with destruction, there is no becoming within it either’ — shows that becoming and destruction are mutually dependent, and that within the flow of arising and ceasing there is no real becoming or destruction. Volume III is thus complete.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Volume III proceeds from the refutation of intrinsic nature to the refutation of becoming and destruction. Entering Volume IV, Nāgārjuna turns his focus to the most exalted of subjects — the Buddha himself, the Tathāgata — to demonstrate that correct Middle Way reasoning leaves nothing untouched. The next chapter, Examination of the Tathāgata, refutes the intrinsic nature of the Tathāgata.

Next: Chapter 22 · Examination of the Tathāgata →
Volume IV · Chapter 22
Ch. 22: Examination of the Tathāgata
Middle Way Wisdom: An Analysis of the Logic and Method for Dispelling Attachment
Chapter Summary

The beginning of Volume IV. This chapter applies correct Middle Way reasoning to examine the most exalted of beings: the Tathāgata. Nāgārjuna’s analysis — ‘The Tathāgata is not the aggregates, nor apart from the aggregates; the aggregates are not in the Tathāgata, nor is the Tathāgata in the aggregates; the Tathāgata does not possess the aggregates — so where, then, is the Tathāgata to be found?’ — shows that the Tathāgata is neither identical to nor separate from the five aggregates. The intrinsic nature of the Tathāgata is thus equally unobtainable, to the point that the nature of the Tathāgata is not different from the nature of the world.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Since the Tathāgata cannot be sought through intrinsic nature, what then of the ‘errors’ — the four inversions of permanence, pleasure, self, and purity — that keep sentient beings caught in the cycle of transmigration? Are these truly existent? The next chapter, Examination of Error, refutes both these inverted views and the afflictions they produce.

Next: Chapter 23 · Examination of Error →
Volume IV · Chapter 23
Ch. 23: Examination of Error
The Ultimate Logic for Deconstructing Affliction: From Victim of One’s Emotions to Player Within Reality
Chapter Summary

With the Tathāgata refuted, this chapter turns to the root of affliction: the four inverted views that mistakenly perceive purity, pleasure, permanence, and self. Nāgārjuna’s analysis — ‘the inversions of the pure and the impure are themselves without intrinsic nature, so how could afflictions arise from these two?’ — shows that the inverted views are not real and afflictions do not truly arise; purity and defilement are, from the outset, unobtainable.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With inverted views and afflictions now shown to be empty, the comprehensive refutation preceding this point has reached its completion. Yet the greatest difficulty still remains: ‘If everything is empty, then would not the Four Noble Truths, spiritual practice and realization, and the Three Jewels all collapse as well?’ The next chapter, Examination of the Four Noble Truths, directly answers this most fundamental challenge.

Next: Chapter 24 · Examination of the Four Noble Truths →
Volume IV · Chapter 24
Ch. 24: Examination of the Four Noble Truths
The Ultimate Dialectic of Emptiness and Existence: The Pivot of the Two Truths and Dependent Origination’s Emptiness
Chapter Summary

This is the most famous and weightiest chapter of the entire treatise. Facing the challenge that ‘if everything is empty, then the Four Noble Truths and the Three Jewels would all collapse,’ Nāgārjuna responds with the doctrine of the Two Truths, and sets forth his most celebrated verses: ‘That which arises dependent on conditions, I declare to be emptiness. It is also a dependent designation, and is itself the meaning of the Middle Way’ and ‘Precisely because there is the meaning of emptiness, all things are able to be established.’ Emptiness does not destroy phenomena — it is precisely because of emptiness that all phenomena can be established at all.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the meaning of emptiness now clarified, the Four Noble Truths and the Three Jewels remain intact. Following from this, what is the nature of the highest state of realization — nirvāṇa? Does it exist? Does it not exist? The next chapter, Examination of Nirvāṇa, refutes the fourfold clinging to nirvāṇa.

Next: Chapter 25 · Examination of Nirvāṇa →
Volume IV · Chapter 25
Ch. 25: Examination of Nirvāṇa
The Logic and Method of Liberation: Nirvāṇa and the World Are Not the Slightest Bit Different
Chapter Summary

With the meaning of emptiness now established, this chapter examines the highest state of realization: nirvāṇa. Nāgārjuna comprehensively refutes the fourfold clinging to nirvāṇa as existent, non-existent, both existent and non-existent, or neither existent nor non-existent, and sets forth the verse: ‘Between nirvāṇa and the world, there is not the slightest bit of difference’ — nirvāṇa is not some separate thing to be attained; it is realized precisely within the true nature of birth and death itself.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

Since nirvāṇa is not apart from the true nature of the world, how then should we contemplate the concrete mechanism by which the world continues to turn — the twelve links of dependent origination? The next chapter, Examination of the Twelve Links, contemplates the twelve links of dependent origination in both forward and reverse order.

Next: Chapter 26 · Examination of the Twelve Links →
Volume IV · Chapter 26
Ch. 26: Examination of the Twelve Links
Transmigration and Cessation: Contemplating the Twelve Links of Dependent Origination in Forward and Reverse Order
Chapter Summary

Unlike the preceding chapters’ method of ‘refutation,’ this chapter directly lays out the mechanism of transmigration through birth and death: the twelve links of dependent origination. Nāgārjuna contemplates the forward order — ‘ignorance conditions formations, formations condition consciousness...’ — to show transmigration, and the reverse order — ‘when ignorance ceases, formations cease...’ — to show cessation, revealing that dependent origination is both the chain that binds and the gateway to liberation.

Looking Back, Looking Forward

With the principle of transmigration and cessation now made clear, the treatise moves toward its final task: clearing away the various ‘wrong views’ that sentient beings hold about the past, the future, the self, and the world. The next chapter, Examination of Views, refutes these extreme views and brings the entire treatise to its conclusion.

Next: Chapter 27 · Examination of Views →
Volume IV · Chapter 27
Ch. 27: Examination of Views
The Middle Way Wisdom That Dismantles All Frameworks, and Its Method of Practice
Chapter Summary

The final chapter of the entire treatise. Nāgārjuna comprehensively refutes the many wrong and extreme views that sentient beings hold concerning ‘the past, the future, the self, and the world’ (the class of conceptual proliferation exemplified by the fourteen unanswerable questions), showing that all views naturally dissolve once dependent origination and emptiness are understood. The treatise closes with a final verse of homage: ‘Gautama, the great sage, out of compassion taught this Dharma, in order to cut off all views entirely — I bow my head before him now.’ With this, the entire treatise is complete.

The Treatise on the Middle Way, in all twenty-seven chapters, is complete here · “Gautama, the great sage, out of compassion taught this Dharma, in order to cut off all views entirely — I bow my head before him now.”
Treatise on the Middle Way · Complete English Reading Edition, All 27 Chapters

Composed by Nāgārjuna Bodhisattva · Commentary by the Brahmin Piṅgala · Translated (into Chinese) by Tripiṭaka Master Kumārajīva of the Later Qin

諸佛依二諦　為眾生說法　一以世俗諦　二第一義諦
以有空義故　一切法得成　若無空義者　一切則不成

This is a personal study compilation, warmly shared as a connection to the Dharma.
願以此功德，普及於一切；
我等與眾生，皆共成佛道。
